sections.paragraph: , שִׁחֹור, שִׁחֹר [Shihor, Sihor], properly “black,” “turbid” (root שָׁחַר), hence pr.n. (in Heb. just as יְאֹר is Egyptian), of the river Nile, so called from its muddy and turbid waters (whence Lat. Melo, i.q. μέλας, according to Festus and Serv. ad Georg. iv. 291; Æn. i. 745, iv. 246). Isa 23:3, זֶרַע שִׁחֹר קְצִיר יְאֹר “the sowing of Shihor, the harvest of the Nile.” (Vulg. Nili.) Jerem. 2:18 . In two places ( Jos 13:3 1Ch 13:5), Shihor is placed on the southern borders of Palestine, where the river of Egypt (נַחַל מִצְרַיִם see נַחַל ) would rather have been expected [but they surely were the same], but in a similar sentence, Gen 15:18, the Nile is also mentioned.
